exemplar.title: Investigation into the influence of an addition of visual stimuli on memory.

detailedExemplarData.title: Investigation into the influence of an addition of visual stimuli on memory.

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Clear and focused aim with explicit relevance to IB student study strategies
Well-integrated background research with Kargopoulos et al. example
weaknesses: Minor inaccuracies in theory description (e.g., spelling, causal mechanism sketchy)
Incomplete operationalisation of variables (timing and scoring criteria not fully detailed)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The introduction states the aim and explains its educational relevance very clearly, satisfying the top band. The theory/model is described and linked but with small errors and limited mechanistic detail, placing it in the 5â6 band at 5. The hypotheses correctly identify and partially operationalise IV and DV, missing full detail, which keeps it just below a perfect score.

strengths: Sampling technique and participant characteristics are identified
weaknesses: Limited explanation or justification for design choice and control of extraneous variables
Materials and controlled variables are described but rationale is not explained
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The exploration section meets the basic descriptive requirements (band 1â2) but lacks sufficient explanation and justification across all strands. While the design, sampling and materials are described, their choices and rationale are not fully explained, warranting 2 out of 4.

strengths: Appropriate choice of descriptive and inferential statistics for small independent samples
Graph addresses the hypothesis and findings are linked back to theory
weaknesses: Implausible p-value reporting and missing test statistics
Graphical presentation lacks error bars, correct scaling and axis labels
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although appropriate statistical tests and a relevant graph are used, significant calculation and reporting errors, along with incomplete interpretation of variability and missing graphical details, place the analysis in the lower half of band 3â4, adjusted to 2 to reflect the severity of errors.

strengths: Findings are discussed with reference to dual-coding theory and prior studies
Modifications are explicitly linked to identified limitations
weaknesses: Evaluation discussion remains surface-level without alternative explanations
Justification for proposed modifications lacks feasibility details
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation identifies relevant strengths, limitations, and linked modifications, aligning with band 3â4 performance. However, the depth of discussion and justification is moderate, meriting 3 out of 6.

strandSelections.reasoning: A bar graph of mean recall scores is included and it relates directly to the hypothesis.  However, axis labels lack units, the y-axis does not start at zero, and no error bars are provided, so the presentation is imperfect.  This aligns with the 3â4 band (âgraph addresses hypothesis but contains errorsâ), meriting 3 marks.

The student states that the null is rejected, quotes the p-value, and notes the direction and size of the mean difference, linking this to dual coding theory.  However, the interpretation ignores the large standard deviations and possible statistical error, and makes sweeping claims (âhypothesis acceptedâ) without confidence intervals.  This satisfies the 3â4 band but not the highest quality, so 3 marks are given.

Both null and research hypotheses are stated and the IV (presence/absence of a photograph) and DV (number of descriptors recalled) are identified.  Operationalisation is partially present: recall is quantified out of 20 statements, but timing and scoring criteria for correctness are not fully detailed.  Because operationalisation is incomplete, the work reaches the 3â4 band.  A score of 4 reflects that most essential components are present and accurate.

The sampling method is described as ârandomly selecting classesâ, and participant characteristics (age, gender distribution, ethnicity) are listed.  However, the explanation of why this technique and group were chosen is minimal and contradictory (claims both low variability and representativeness).  The strand therefore meets the 1â2 band, earning the upper mark of 2 for providing basic descriptive detail.

Paivioâs dual-coding theory is described with its two channels and predicted memory advantage, and Kargopoulos et al. (2003) is summarised as supporting evidence.  However, the description contains some inaccuracies (e.g., spelling of Paivio, confusion over âverbal system onlyâ for names) and the causal mechanism is sketched rather than fully elaborated.  The link to the present investigation is present but not deeply analysedâhence the strand earns the 3â4 band, placed at the upper end because both description and link are nonetheless clear.

Findings are discussed in relation to Paivioâs theory and Kargopoulos et al. study, demonstrating conceptual linkage rather than simple description.  The discussion remains surface-level (e.g., limited depth on why effect size is modest) and does not integrate contradicting evidence, so the work sits in the middle of the 3â4 band, earning 3 marks.

The student clearly states the aim â to examine whether adding a photograph to verbal descriptors enhances recall â and explicitly links its relevance to improving study methods for IB students.  This practical educational angle goes beyond merely naming a context and therefore fulfils the rubric requirement for an âexplainedâ relevance.  Minor wording slips do not detract from a solid, well-focused explanation, so the work fits comfortably in the 5â6 band; a mid-band score of 5 is appropriate.

Several strengths (independent design) and limitations (small, biased sample; procedural inconsistencies) are identified and each is followed by a brief explanation of its impact on validity or generalisability.  The coverage is relevant but not in-depth, matching the descriptor for âstated, described and relevantâ; hence 3 marks within the 3â4 band.

Materials are itemised and a few controlled elements (same descriptors, distraction task) are named, but there is little justification for the choice of those materials or discussion of other potential extraneous variables.  As control and rationale are only described, not explained, the strand sits in the 1â2 band; 2 marks are given because some control is evident.

Two clear modifications (online proctored delivery; broader recruiting) are linked to identified limitations of sampling bias and procedural inconsistency and are partially justified.  Further justification (feasibility, specific methodological details) is thin, so the response fulfils but does not excel in the âexplicitly linked and justifiedâ requirement.  Consequently, it gains the lower end (3) of the 3â4 band.

The independent-samples design is identified and the student briefly explains why it allows comparison between conditions.  Some rationale (same list of characteristics for both groups) is given, but there is no consideration of alternative designs or control of order effects, making the explanation limited rather than thorough.  This fits the lower end of the âexplainedâ 3â4 band; 3 marks are awarded.

Mean, standard deviation and a Mann-Whitney U test are reported, which are appropriate for two independent, small samples of ordinal/interval data.  Nevertheless, the extremely small p-value (0.00021) is implausible for n=11 per group, suggesting a calculation or reporting error, and test statistics are not shown.  Thus statistics are âappropriate but contain errorsâ, fitting the 3â4 band; 3 marks are awarded.

annotations.comment: Operationalisation of the DV notes â20 descriptors recalled,â but lacks criteria for scoring correctness (e.g., exact wording, synonyms). Define scoring rules to ensure replicability.
Research design is described as independent samples, but explanation of how it controls for individual differences or order effects is limited. Discuss potential design alternatives.
Ethical procedures are thorough, covering consent and debrief. Consider also addressing data privacy measures (e.g., data storage, anonymisation) to strengthen ethical rigor.
Sampling description notes random class selection and demographics. However, the rationale for choosing IB DP students is minimal; further justify representativeness and control of sampling bias.
Materials are listed comprehensively but the control of extraneous variables (e.g., familiarity with characters) is not justified. Explain why each material choice minimises confounds.
The reported p-value (<0.00022) seems implausibly small for n=11 per group, indicating a possible calculation or reporting error. Recompute or justify the statistical output.
Aim and relevance are well articulated, explicitly linking to IB student study strategies. This clear statement addresses both investigation aim and educational relevance.

The description of dual coding theory is clear but could be deepened by elaborating how cross-referential encoding specifically enhances recall in your context. Consider detailing the mechanism by which pictorial information engages both verbal and nonverbal channels.

Strong integration of Kargopoulos et al. (2003) study provides empirical foundation. Including sample size, methodology, and statistical significance demonstrates good application of background research.
Hypotheses are clearly stated with IV and DV identified. To strengthen, fully operationalise variables by specifying measurement timing and scoring thresholds.
Hypotheses are clear but do not specify the alpha level defining significance. Stating the criterion (e.g., p<0.05) in hypothesis would clarify decision rule.
Given the small sample of 11 per condition, consider conducting an a priori power analysis to determine if sample size is adequate to detect the expected effect.
Procedure outlines steps clearly. To further standardise, describe researcher scripts or timing controls to reduce presentation inconsistencies.
The descriptive table correctly displays means and SDs. To support analysis transparency, include test statistics (e.g., U value) alongside p-values.
Procedure limitations and proposed proctored online software are linked to extraneous variables. Justify feasibility and specific impact to strengthen the modification rationale.
Limitation of low participant variability is relevant and well explained, including demographic breakdown. This addresses strengths and limitations effectively.
The conclusion cautiously addresses generalizability issues. To enhance, summarise key findings concisely and relate to educational applications explicitly.
Interpretation notes mean difference and SDs but omits discussion of confidence intervals or effect size. Including these would strengthen findings interpretation.
No mention of data distribution or homogeneity assumptions for Mann-Whitney U test. Report tests for normality or justify nonparametric choice.
Graph caption is present, but graph itself lacks axis labels, units, and error bars. Ensure graphical elements accurately represent variability and scale.
Figure 2 visually addresses the hypothesis. Improve clarity by labelling axes with units, starting y-axis at zero, and adding error bars.
Discussion links findings back to dual coding theory and original study. To deepen evaluation, integrate alternative explanations or conflicting results from literature.
The evaluation of design contrasts with original study design, highlighting temporal differences. This reflection demonstrates critical appraisal of methodological choices.
Modifications of digital administration and proctoring are stated. However, link each specific modification directly to identified limitation for maximal justification.
